10-18-2024, 01:50 PM
ozmoe571
889 posts, read
1,073,467

Reputation: 1958
Advertisements
North Carolina has a new first day early voting record set. A total of 353,166 voters turned out statewide, according to data from the State Board of Elections. That tops the previous record for the first day of early voting – 348,559, set in 2020 – by 1.3 percent.
Wake County saw the greatest number of first-day ballots cast at 30,843, while almost 30,000 voters cast ballots in Mecklenburg County.
https://www.wral.com/north-carolina-...&ICID=ref_fark

Doesn't surprise me at all. Moreover, today looked to be every bit as busy as yesterday at my local polling location. Traffic backed up for half a mile in both directions, and long lines of people lucky enough to get a parking place. I plan to wait until next week and try again.

Location: Washington, DC
224 posts, read

Reputation: 421
NC early voting makes me feel pessimistic about Dems taking the state given the makeup of the early votes.
I know things are different post covid in early voting but man it’s like 30/30/30 GOP/Dem/Other. Other important swing states are much less GOP it seems. I think AZ & NV are doing quite strong for the GOP as well.
Any insight on why that is? Did a bunch of Dems switch to unaffiliated to vote Haley in the GOP primaries potentially?
Any personal anecdotes on what the political climate is like in NC?
Edit: Charlotte & Raleigh’s respective counties aren’t broken out by political affiliation in a widely used source
https://projects.votehub.us/pages/early-voting-tracker
Last edited by NorthAmerica_US; 10-18-2024 at
04:46 PM

Any personal anecdotes on what the political climate is like in NC?
Edit: Charlotte & Raleigh’s respective counties aren’t broken out by political affiliation in a widely used source
https://projects.votehub.us/pages/early-voting-tracker
I don't know exactly why you're feeling this way or where you're getting your information from, but based on the voting from 2020, the Democrats probably are feeling pretty good about the early voting in North Carolina. Per Politico, the top two counties with the most ballots on the first day of early voting, Wake and Mecklenberg, went decisively for Biden in 2020. In Wake, it was 62.5% Biden vs. 35.9% Trump. In Mecklenberg, the split was even bigger for Biden, 66.9% to 31.7% for Trump.
And I can't imagine that given the most recent elections where Democrats have maintained control of local offices that there would be a sudden upswing in favor of Trump.
Now, of course, Wake and Mecklenberg made up only a fifth of the first day early voting tally, and we've got more than two weeks left before polling ends, so anything could happen. Still, by this record turnout with solid number in many urban counties that lean Democratic, plus the drag that Robinson is putting on the Republican side with his baggage running for governor, I'd be pretty nervous right now if I were on the GOP side.
Source:
https://www.politico.com/2020-electi...orth-carolina/

Some Republicans and many Independents will vote Harris this year.
North Carolina has been mostly leaning Republican for years. It only went blue in 2008 for Obama, in recent years.
So the fact Harris can flip it, in and of itself, would be huge if it happens.

A friend of mine works for a company headquartered in North Carolina, and I work for a company that has a large office in North Carolina. We both saw quite a few OOO alerts yesterday from people leaving early to participate in the early voting. (We neither one know what their political affiliations are, just for the record.)

Quote:
Originally Posted by
jjbradleynyc
This is huge! Glad to see.
Higher the numbers, higher the likelihood Harris will win.
Is the conventional wisdom that "turnout helps Dems" still true? Or is there a silent majority that is super pi$$ed off about what this administration does and stands for?
I rather think it might be the latter. The largest cohort of Millennials turns 34 this year. That's prime house buying/kid having age and while some of them will remain loyal Bolsheviks their entire lives (like you), finally growing up has a way of making you take stock of early beliefs. Every generation before has become more conservative over time, why should they be any different, and there are a LOT of them.
There comes a time for most of us to put away childish things, again, you excepted.

Here in Arizona used to be 88% of all voters voted early by mail that dropped to mid 70% range after Trump said not to use mail in voting. That's why early voting shows republicans voting in greater numbers then democrats more republicans. We also have non party like myself who make up 1/3 of the registered voters. I believe many non party voters are Harris voters like myself who left the Arizona republican party after Trump lost the 2020 election attempted to overturn the results.
